title: Heat interface unit maintenance for district heat connections
subtitle: Cleaning filters, recognising faults, and understanding HIU metering on a district heating network
excerpt: A heat interface unit (HIU) is the connection point between a district heating network and a flat or house. It contains a heat exchanger, pressure regulators, and a heat meter. Most faults that residents experience with district heating are HIU problems, not network problems. This explains the monthly checks and what to report to the heat network operator.
sourceNotes: Heat Trust scheme guidance on HIU maintenance requirements for heat network operators. CIBSE TM65 guidance on district heating connections. BEIS heat network guidance for consumers. Useful Simple Technologies (UST) HIU documentation (publicly available).

content.text: A heat interface unit sits in a utility cupboard or on the wall of a flat and contains everything that connects the flat's heating and hot water to the district network. The network delivers hot water at typically 70-80°C; the HIU transfers that heat across a heat exchanger to the flat's heating and domestic hot water circuits. The resident pays for the heat consumed, measured by the HIU's heat meter in kWh.
The HIU is generally serviced annually by the heat network operator or a contractor they appoint. But residents can do monthly checks that catch problems early and avoid unnecessary no-heat callouts.

Check the system pressure gauge. Most HIUs show the domestic circuit pressure on a dial or digital display. Normal operating pressure: 1.0-1.5 bar cold. Below 0.7 bar: the circuit needs repressurising. Above 2.0 bar: the expansion vessel may be waterlogged.

Check the strainer filter. The HIU's primary (network) inlet has a strainer that catches debris from the network water. The filter housing is usually a transparent bowl or a lockable cap on the primary connection. If visible debris has accumulated, this needs clearing; contact the network operator for the cleaning procedure.
Note the heat meter reading. Record the meter reading monthly. Compare with your heat bill. If the metered kWh and the billed kWh diverge, report the discrepancy to the operator.

Check for visible leaks or damp around the unit. A small damp patch under the HIU is usually condensation on the cold domestic circuit pipework. A continuously wet patch under the primary side indicates a seal failure; report immediately.
Common faults and what they mean
No heat or hot water, network supply confirmed good: the HIU's flow-control valve or the heat exchanger may be blocked. This is an engineer call.
Hot water runs cold after a few seconds, then warm again: a thermostatic mixing valve fault or a heat exchanger with partial fouling. The HIU cannot meet demand for more than a short flow.

Heat meter shows use in summer when heating is off: the hot-water loop may be kept running to prevent legionella, or the meter may be faulty. Ask the network operator if they charge for this loop heat.

District heat bills have two components: a standing charge (per day, covering network maintenance and capital) and a unit charge (per kWh consumed). Standing charges on older networks are often higher than on newer ones; they were set to recover infrastructure costs and may not reflect current cost. If you have a Heat Trust tariff review right (most residents signed up since 2020 do), request a tariff breakdown annually.

Heat networks typically have lower carbon intensity than gas boilers because the heat source includes waste heat, biomass, or CHP. But the price per kWh is often higher than gas, particularly on older networks with high standing charges. The total cost depends heavily on the specific network and standing charge structure.

attrs.cause: Codes vary by manufacturer; common codes relate to no-flow from the network, low system pressure, or sensor faults
attrs.fix: Note the exact code and look it up in the manufacturer's HIU manual (usually available on their website). For no-flow codes, check with the network operator whether there is a known supply interruption before calling an engineer.

attrs.symptom: Metered consumption is unexpectedly high compared to a similar property on the same network
attrs.cause: A stuck-open heat-exchanger valve is drawing network heat even when the thermostat is calling for no heat
attrs.fix: Report to the operator and ask for an HIU inspection. A valve fault can double annual heat consumption.
